entities.name: sādhutvajñāna
entities.cite: Its visaya: sadhutva-jnana, knowledge of which forms are correct

entities.name: śiṣṭa
entities.cite: usage of the learned as criterion

entities.name: sādhutva
entities.cite: correctness as such, the dharma-bearing property

axioms.cite: grammar as a *smrti*, a remembrance-tradition, not a sruti

axioms.name: visaya
axioms.cite: Its visaya: sadhutva-jnana, knowledge of which forms are correct

axioms.name: nibandhana
axioms.cite: Its ground: avicchedena sistanam — the *unbrokenness* of the cultivated line, idam smrti-nibandhanam, this is what fastens the remembrance

axioms.name: dharma_bearing
axioms.cite: correctness as such, the dharma-bearing property

axioms.name: avicchinna
axioms.cite: the chain of cultivated speakers never broke

claims.name: visaya
claims.cite: whose object is the knowledge of correctness

claims.name: nibandhana
claims.cite: grounded in the unbroken remembrance of the cultivated

rejected_readings.label: meaning_conflation
rejected_readings.rendering: The grammatical tradition studies the meaning conveyed by correct forms.
rejected_readings.why: The verse specifies the object is knowledge of correctness (sadhutva-jnana), not meaning. The commentary emphasizes that corrupt forms convey meaning equally (1.27), so meaning cannot be what uniquely defines grammar. This misinterprets the visaya (object/domain) of the tradition.

rejected_readings.label: sruti_confusion
rejected_readings.rendering: Grammar is a revealed tradition (sruti) directly communicated by the gods.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly distinguishes grammar as a smrti (remembrance-tradition) from sruti (revealed tradition). Grammar is derivative of the Veda through secondary markers (lingas), not revealed directly. This reverses the established classification of grammar's epistemic status.

notes: The verse defines grammar (vyakarana-smrti) as a remembrance-tradition grounded in the unbroken continuity of cultivated speakers, whose domain is the knowledge of correctness. The contract encodes the three core relations: visaya (object), nibandhana (ground), and the identity with smrti. Sistas as power reflects their role as foundational epistemic authority in the commentary.
